macro_segments.title: Introduction and Review of Prior Cases on Single Witness Testimony

micro_segments.title: Overview of Rabbinic Innovation Allowing Single Witness Testimony for Death
Review of Prior Mishna on One Against One or One Against Two Testimony
Two Models: Ulla's Model of Testimony vs. Rabbi Nechemiah's Model of Evidence

macro_segments.title: Mishna on Co-wives: One Says Husband Died, One Says He Did Not Die

micro_segments.title: Case of Two Co-wives with Contradictory Testimony on Husband's Death
Why the Co-wife Cannot Testify or Marry: Rejection of Co-wife Testimony

macro_segments.title: Case of Died vs. Killed: Rabbi Meir's Position on Contradictory Specifics

micro_segments.title: Mishna: One Wife Says Husband Died, Another Says Husband Was Murdered
Rabbi Meir's Position: Both Accounts Contradict and Neutralize
Rabbi Yehuda and Rabbi Shimon: Both Agree He Died, So Both Can Marry

macro_segments.title: Mishna on Co-wives: One Says Husband Died, One Says He Did Not Die (continued)

micro_segments.title: Gemara: Reasons for Rejecting Co-wife's Testimony and the Shimshon Analogy
Discussion of Chidusha: First Wife Can Marry Despite Co-wife's Contradiction

macro_segments.title: Case of Died vs. Killed: Rabbi Meir's Position on Contradictory Specifics (continued)

micro_segments.title: Gemara Debate: Rabbi Elazar vs. Rabbi Yochanan on Rabbi Meir's Intent
Resolution: Co-wife's Claim That Husband Did Not Die Is Worthless

macro_segments.title: Mishna: Woman Returns from Overseas and Testifies Husband Died

micro_segments.title: Classic Case: Woman and Husband Go Overseas; She Returns Testifying His Death
Co-wife Cannot Remarry; Debate of Rabbi Tarfon and Rabbi Akiva
Case of Mother-in-law: Woman Testifies Husband Died Then Father-in-Law Died
Gemara Clarification: Both Cases Needed to Show Different Standards

macro_segments.title: Case of Marrying One of Five Women: Uncertainty and Obligation to Pay

micro_segments.title: Scenario: Man Marries One of Five Women but Does Not Know Which One
Legal Framework: Hamotzi Mechaveiro and Doubt vs. Certainty
Rabbi Tarfon: No Special Obligation Beyond Depositing Ketuba Money
Rabbi Akiva: Must Pay Each Wife Separately; Moral vs. Legal Obligation

macro_segments.title: Parallel Case of Stealing from One of Five People

micro_segments.title: Scenario: Man Stole from One of Five People but Does Not Know Which
Difference Between Marriage Case and Theft Case: Heightened Moral Obligation
Gemara: Rabbi Shimon ben Elazar Distinguishes When Debate Applies
Clarification: Debate Only Applies to Cases with Special Heightened Obligation

macro_segments.title: Complex Cases of Woman Returning with Son and Yibum Status

micro_segments.title: Woman Returns: Testifies Husband Died, Then Son Died (Neutralizes Her Own Statement)
Woman Testifies: Son Died First (Claims Yibum Obligation)
Woman Returns with Husband and Brother-in-law; All Go Overseas
Woman Testifies to Events Overseas About Brother-in-law or New Son
Scope of Woman's Believability: Only About Husband, Not Sister or Brother-in-law

macro_segments.title: Gemara Discussion: Can Husband Give Get to Wife Not Present Through Third Party

micro_segments.title: Question: Can Husband Appoint Agent to Give Get to Wife to Avoid Yibum
Principle of Mezakeh: Can You Merit Someone Without Their Presence or Knowledge
Two Positions: Benefit to Wife (To Avoid Yibum) vs. Potential Harm (Wife Loves Brother-in-law)
Example: Can Husband Give Get if Wife and Husband Are Fighting
Reish Lakish: Better to Sit as Two Than Sit as Widow (Women Prefer Marriage)
Tannaitic Statements: Women Settle for Poor Husbands Rather Than Be Unmarried
Braita Reversal: But Women Also Commit Adultery When Unhappy in Marriage
Conclusion and Modern Implications for Get Refusal and Abuse Cases

topical_tags.term: Ulla vs. Rabbi Nechemiah (models of testimony)
Rabbi Meir vs. Rabbi Yehuda/Shimon (died vs. killed)
Rabbi Tarfon vs. Rabbi Akiva (obligation to pay)
Moral vs. legal obligation (דיני שמים vs. דיני אדם)
Rabbi Shimon ben Elazar (distinction of cases)
Shalom bayit (family peace) and its limits
